assets/gallery/photos/Day1/day1-01.jpg.caption: Pic 1: This exhibit from the POLIN Museum displays a symbolic key, representing the homes, communities, and personal histories that shaped Jewish life in Poland.

assets/gallery/photos/Day1/day1-02.jpg.caption: Pic 2: This room in the POLIN Museum recreates the look and atmosphere of Jewish cultural life in Poland, helping visitors understand the beauty, tradition, and everyday experiences of Jewish communities before World War II.

assets/gallery/photos/Day1/day1-03.jpg.caption: Pic 3: This exhibit highlights everyday Jewish life in Poland, including markets, food, trade, and community traditions that were an important part of Jewish culture before the Holocaust.

assets/gallery/photos/Day1/day1-04.jpg.caption: Pic 4: Located outside the POLIN Museum, the Monument to the Ghetto Heroes honors the courage and resistance of Jewish fighters during the Warsaw Ghetto Uprising. Its placement near the museum connects the memory of loss and resistance with the larger history of Jewish life in Poland.

assets/gallery/photos/Day1/day1-05.jpg.caption: Pic 5: This POLIN Museum exhibit shows a historic map of a Jewish community in Poland, highlighting how Jewish life was deeply connected to cities, neighborhoods, and local culture. It helps visitors understand that Jewish history in Poland was built through real communities, not just major historical events.

assets/gallery/photos/Day1/day1-06.jpg.caption: Pic 6: This POLIN Museum exhibit shows portraits of Polish rulers, connecting Jewish history to the larger political history of Poland. It highlights how Jewish communities lived within changing governments, laws, and societies over time.

assets/gallery/photos/Day1/day1-07.jpg.caption: Pic 7: This display uses artwork and a model to show the movement and development of Jewish communities in Poland. It helps explain how Jewish life grew through migration, settlement, trade, and connection to local towns.

assets/gallery/photos/Day1/day1-08.jpg.caption: Pic 8: This exhibit focuses on Jewish culture in modern Poland, including music, theater, and public life. It shows that Jewish history is not only about religion or tragedy, but also about creativity, expression, and identity.

assets/gallery/photos/Day1/day1-09.jpg.caption: Pic 9: This artifact display helps connect visitors to the material culture of Jewish communities in Poland. Objects like this show how history can be understood through the items people used, valued, and preserved.

assets/gallery/photos/Day1/day1-10.jpg.caption: Pic 10: This model of a synagogue at the POLIN Museum shows the importance of religious and community spaces in Jewish life. It helps visitors understand how synagogues served not only as places of worship, but also as centers of identity, learning, and connection.

assets/gallery/photos/Day1/day1-11.jpg.caption: Pic 11: This exhibit highlights an important historical figure connected to Jewish life in Poland. By including individual stories and leaders, the POLIN Museum shows that Jewish history was shaped by real people, choices, and experiences.

assets/gallery/photos/Day1/day1-12.jpg.caption: Pic 12: This photo shows the Palace of Culture and Science in Warsaw, one of the city’s most recognizable landmarks. Its presence in the city reflects Poland’s complex modern history and the way Warsaw continues to rebuild, remember, and move forward.

assets/gallery/photos/Day1/day1-13.jpg.caption: Pic 13: This reconstructed synagogue ceiling is one of the most powerful displays in the POLIN Museum. Its detailed colors and artwork show the beauty, creativity, and spiritual life that existed within Jewish communities in Poland.

assets/gallery/photos/Day1/day1-14.jpg.caption: Pic 14: This display features religious objects connected to Jewish tradition and worship. Artifacts like these help preserve the memory of Jewish faith, culture, and community life across generations.

assets/gallery/photos/Day1/day1-16.jpg.caption: Pic 16: This library exhibit at the POLIN Museum highlights the importance of Jewish learning, scholarship, and religious study. The quote above the shelves, “Your book alone made me into a man,” shows how deeply books and education shaped Jewish identity and intellectual life.

assets/gallery/photos/Day1/day1-17.jpg.caption: Pic 17: This theater-style exhibit shows how film and performance became part of Jewish cultural life in Poland. It reminds visitors that Jewish history includes creativity, entertainment, and artistic expression, not only tragedy.

assets/gallery/photos/Day1/day1-18.jpg.caption: Pic 18: This poster reflects efforts to promote literacy and education in Polish society. Within the museum, it connects to the broader theme of how knowledge, language, and public education shaped communities and cultural identity.

assets/gallery/photos/Day1/day1-19.jpg.caption: Pic 19: This nighttime view of Warsaw shows the city beyond the museum, connecting our visit to the modern life of Poland today. It reminds us that Warsaw is a place where history, memory, and everyday life exist side by side.

assets/gallery/photos/Day1/day1-20.jpg.caption: Pic 20: This exhibit uses symbols and artwork to show how Jewish communities were represented within Polish history. It helps visitors understand the connections between identity, heritage, and the visual language used to tell history.

assets/gallery/photos/Day1/day1-21.jpg.caption: Pic 21: This display shows how Jewish migration and settlement were part of a larger European story. The artwork and map help explain how communities moved, adapted, and built lives in new places.

assets/gallery/photos/Day1/day1-22.jpg.caption: Pic 22: Entrance of the POLIN museum.

assets/gallery/photos/Day2/day2-05.jpg.caption: Pic 5: The Palace of Culture and Science, built after World War II, stands near the former Warsaw Ghetto and symbolizes the city's reconstruction following the devastation of the Holocaust.

assets/gallery/photos/Day2/day2-06.jpg.caption: Pic 6: “Ulica Raoula Wallenberga” means “Raoul Wallenberg Street.” It commemorates Raoul Wallenberg, the Swedish diplomat who saved tens of thousands of Hungarian Jews during the Holocaust by issuing protective passports and establishing safe houses in Budapest.

assets/gallery/photos/Day2/day2-13.jpg.caption: Pic 13: The Monument to the Ghetto Heroes commemorates the Jewish fighters and civilians of the Warsaw Ghetto and honors those who resisted Nazi persecution.

assets/gallery/photos/Day2/day2-14.jpg.caption: Pic 14: Built after World War II, the Palace of Culture and Science overlooks areas once devastated during the Holocaust and symbolizes Warsaw's postwar reconstruction.

assets/gallery/photos/Day2/day2-15.jpg.caption: Pic 15: Warsaw's modern skyline reflects the city's rebuilding after the destruction brought by World War II and the Holocaust.

assets/gallery/photos/Day2/day2-16.jpg.caption: Pic 16: This monument honors Janusz Korczak, the educator who chose to remain with the children of his orphanage and accompanied them from the Warsaw Ghetto to their deaths during the Holocaust.

assets/gallery/photos/Day2/day2-17.jpg.caption: Pic 17: Another look at the Monument to the Ghetto Heroes in Warsaw honors the Jewish fighters and victims of the Warsaw Ghetto and commemorates the 1943 Warsaw Ghetto Uprising.

assets/gallery/photos/Day2/day2-18.jpg.caption: Pic 18: These steel structures mark the location of the wooden footbridge that connected the two sections of the Warsaw Ghetto, allowing victims to cross above Chłodna Street while remaining confined within the ghetto.

assets/gallery/photos/Day2/day2-19.jpg.caption: Pic 19: The night sky above the former Warsaw Ghetto serves as a reminder of the importance of preserving the memory of the Jewish community that once flourished in Warsaw.

assets/gallery/photos/Day2/day2-20.jpg.caption: Pic 20: The Westin Warsaw hotel stands in the heart of a city rebuilt after the devastation of World War II and the Holocaust, reflecting Warsaw's transformation into a modern European capital.

assets/gallery/photos/Day3/day3-01.jpg.caption: Pic 1: Entrance and general view of the Majdanek Concentration Camp

assets/gallery/photos/Day3/day3-02.jpg.caption: Pic 2: Entrance sign to the Prisoners of Majdanek exhibition

assets/gallery/photos/Day3/day3-03.jpg.caption: Pic 3: Sculpture of a tortoise created by prisoner Albin Maria Boniecki

assets/gallery/photos/Day3/day3-04.jpg.caption: Pic 4: Camp clothing belonging to Helena Kurcyusz, including shoes, a striped jacket, and a headscarf

assets/gallery/photos/Day3/day3-05.jpg.caption: Pic 5: Close-up view of the camp clothing belonging to Helena Kurcyusz

assets/gallery/photos/Day3/day3-06.jpg.caption: Pic 6: Photographs recovered from the grounds of the Majdanek Concentration Camp

assets/gallery/photos/Day3/day3-07.jpg.caption: Pic 7: Art objects created by prisoners at the Majdanek Concentration Camp

assets/gallery/photos/Day3/day3-08.jpg.caption: Pic 8: Biography of Jerzy Pfeffer, a survivor of the Majdanek Concentration Camp

assets/gallery/photos/Day3/day3-09.jpg.caption: Pic 9: Drawing of a carpenter created by Helena Kurcyusz

assets/gallery/photos/Day3/day3-10.jpg.caption: Pic 10: Cross given to Bronisława Korzeniowska (née Milewska) by Amour Collin

assets/gallery/photos/Day3/day3-11.jpg.caption: Pic 11: Shoes confiscated from prisoners upon their arrival at the Majdanek Concentration Camp

assets/gallery/photos/Day3/day3-12.jpg.caption: Pic 12: Overcrowded and unhygienic bunk beds in the prisoners’ barracks at the Majdanek Concentration Camp

assets/gallery/photos/Day3/day3-13.jpg.caption: Pic 13: Memorial exhibit at the Majdanek Concentration Camp museum featuring a fragment of a camp road constructed from stones taken from Jewish tombstones (matzevot)

assets/gallery/photos/Day3/day3-14.jpg.caption: Pic 14: Watchtower at the Majdanek Concentration Camp used by Nazi officials to observe inmates

assets/gallery/photos/Day3/day3-15.jpg.caption: Pic 15: The Column of the Three Eagles, the first monument to the victims of the Majdanek Concentration Camp

assets/gallery/photos/Day3/day3-16.jpg.caption: Pic 16: Description of the Column of the Three Eagles monument at the Majdanek Concentration Camp

assets/gallery/photos/Day3/day3-17.jpg.caption: Pic 17: Side view of the camp grounds at the Majdanek Concentration Camp

assets/gallery/photos/Day3/day3-18.jpg.caption: Pic 18: The Mausoleum at the Majdanek Concentration Camp, inscribed with “Los nasz dla was przestrogą” (“Our fate is a warning to you”)

assets/gallery/photos/Day3/day3-19.jpg.caption: Pic 19: Side view of the Mausoleum

assets/gallery/photos/Day3/day3-20.jpg.caption: Pic 20: Crematorium at the Majdanek Concentration Camp

assets/gallery/photos/Day3/day3-21.jpg.caption: Pic 21: This memorial stone at the Majdanek Concentration Camp in Lublin commemorates the approximately 18,400 Jewish prisoners murdered on November 3, 1943, during Operation Harvest Festival (Erntefest), whose bodies were buried in trenches nearby, and serves today as a site of remembrance for the victims of the Holocaust.

assets/gallery/photos/Day4/1.jpg.caption: Pic 1: View from the outside of Belzec, one of many Nazi death camps where Jews were sent to be exterminated

assets/gallery/photos/Day4/2.jpg.caption: Pic 2: Path that Jews would walk on as they approached the gas chambers in Belzec

assets/gallery/photos/Day4/3.jpg.caption: Pic 3: View from the end of the path that Jews would walk on as they approached the gas chambers in Belzec

assets/gallery/photos/Day4/4.jpg.caption: Pic 4: Memorial to the Jews lost in Belzec, as well as a call to action

assets/gallery/photos/Day4/5.jpg.caption: Pic 5: The rubble of Belzec, which was left there so that the camp’s existence and the atrocities committed couldn’t be erased and forgotten

assets/gallery/photos/Day4/6.JPG.caption: Pic 6: Railroad tracks stacked on top of each other that were part of the railroad that brought Jews to Belzec

assets/gallery/photos/Day4/7.JPG.caption: Pic 7: View of shoes within the Belzec museum that were taken from the Jews processed and killed there

assets/gallery/photos/Day4/8.JPG.caption: Pic 8: View of moat surrounding Zamość Fortress

assets/gallery/photos/Day4/9.jpg.caption: Pic 9: View down a tunnel connected Zamość Fortress

assets/gallery/photos/Day4/10.jpg.caption: Pic 10: View down a street of Zamość

assets/gallery/photos/Day4/11.JPG.caption: Pic 11: View down another street of Zamość

assets/gallery/photos/Day4/12.jpg.caption: Pic 12: Torah scroll left behind by traveling Jews during the Nazis advance that was eventually donated to a synagogue (might need to correct me I cant remember exactly)

assets/gallery/photos/Day4/13.jpg.caption: Pic 13: Memorial of Allan Gerson, son of Holocaust survivors and attorney.

assets/gallery/photos/Day4/14.JPG.caption: Pic 14: View of Torah scroll within a synagogue in Zamość

assets/gallery/photos/Day4/15.jpg.caption: Pic 15: Buildings in the Great Market Square in Zamość

assets/gallery/photos/Day4/17.JPG.caption: Pic 17: View down a street in the Great Market Square in Zamość

assets/gallery/photos/Day4/18.JPG.caption: Pic 18: (I don’t know the context of this statue) next to the Parish of the Resurrection of the Lord and St. Thomas the Apostle in Zamość

assets/gallery/photos/Day4/19.jpg.caption: Pic 19: Statue of St. John Paul II, who was pope from 1978 to 2005, outside of the Parish of the Resurrection of the Lord and St. Thomas the Apostle in Zamość

assets/gallery/photos/Day4/20.jpg.caption: Pic 20: View of the sunset near the Zamość Fortress

assets/gallery/photos/Day6/day6-01.jpg.caption: Pic 1: Bird in the moat of Łańcut Castle

assets/gallery/photos/Day6/day6-02.jpg.caption: Pic 2: Bird in the field surrounding Łańcut Castle

assets/gallery/photos/Day6/day6-05.jpg.caption: Pic 5: A 3-branched menorah in Łańcut Synagogue

assets/gallery/photos/Day6/day6-06.jpg.caption: Pic 6: A 4-branched menorah in Łańcut Synagogue

assets/gallery/photos/Day6/day6-07.jpg.caption: Pic 7: Torn shreds of Jewish writing in Łańcut Synagogue

assets/gallery/photos/Day6/day6-08.jpg.caption: Pic 8: Embroidered banner with Hebrew writing in Łańcut Synagogue

assets/gallery/photos/Day6/day6-10.jpg.caption: Pic 10: Another view of 4-branched menorah in Łańcut Synagogue

assets/gallery/photos/Day6/day6-11.jpg.caption: Pic 11: View of the interior of Łańcut Synagogue

assets/gallery/photos/Day6/day6-12.jpg.caption: Pic 12: View of the center structure of the Łańcut Synagogue

assets/gallery/photos/Day6/day6-13.jpg.caption: Pic 13: View of the center structure and centerpiece of the Łańcut Synagogue

assets/gallery/photos/Day6/day6-14.jpg.caption: Pic 14: View of a ceiling painting in Łańcut Synagogue

assets/gallery/photos/Day6/day6-15.jpg.caption: Pic 15: Tablet with Hebrew writing found in the Łańcut Synagogue

assets/gallery/photos/Day8/day8-3563.jpg.caption: Pic 1: This image is the remains of a chimney from a building in the Auschwitz concentration camp.

assets/gallery/photos/Day8/day8-3565.jpg.caption: Pic 2: This is an iron chair that is located in Kraków. There are around 70 chairs scattered in the Ghetto Heroes Square. Each chair symbolizes 1,000 Jewish victims who were taken to the death camps during the Holocaust.

assets/gallery/photos/Day8/day8-3567.jpg.caption: Pic 3: This is an image of prisoners markings.

assets/gallery/photos/Day8/day8-3570.jpg.caption: Pic 4: This is an image of prisoners markings.

assets/gallery/photos/Day8/day8-3572.jpg.caption: Pic 5: This image contains a memorial plaque for those who died and fell victim to the inhumane slaughter of the Jews during the Holocaust.

assets/gallery/photos/Day8/day8-4476.jpg.caption: Pic 6: This image was taken at the Auschwitz concentration camp. These were the bathrooms the prisoners used while they were in the camp.

assets/gallery/photos/Day8/day8-4477.jpg.caption: Pic 7: This image shows the ruins of a building that was located in the Auschwitz concentration camp.

assets/gallery/photos/Day8/day8-4478.jpg.caption: Pic 8: The entrance of the Auschwitz concentration camp.

assets/gallery/photos/Day8/day8-4479.jpg.caption: Pic 9: The “death wall” was where prisoners were lined up to be shot. In the image you can see a tribute to the people who lost their lives at the wall.

assets/gallery/photos/Day8/day8-4480.jpg.caption: Pic 10: “The Book of Names,” thousands of the victims’ names are written on the pages of this book.

assets/gallery/photos/Day8/day8-4482.jpg.caption: Pic 11: This image shows pots and pans that used to belong to the people who were taken into the concentration camp.

assets/gallery/photos/Day8/day8-4485.jpg.caption: Pic 12: This image shows people’s belongings that were stripped away from them once they got to the camps.

assets/gallery/photos/Day8/day8-4487.jpg.caption: Pic 13: This is an image that was taken at the Auschwitz concentration camp. There are hundreds of broken glasses, which belonged to the victims who were taken into the camp.

assets/gallery/photos/Day8/day8-4488.jpg.caption: Pic 14: In the image you can see places where people were taken and sent to Auschwitz.
